docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text: Opus-MT is a project closely associated with the OPUS corpora, dedicated to the distribution of a vast collection of open-source Neural Machine Translation (NMT) models. These models are fully compatible and can be deployed directly via the MTUOC-server.

docs.title: Further Reading
docs.text:
For a comprehensive understanding of the project's architecture and its mission to broaden access to translation technology, please refer to the following publication:

Reference: Tiedemann, J., Aulamo, M., Bakshandaeva, D., Boggia, M., Grönroos, S. A., Nieminen, T., ... & Virpioja, S. (2024). Democratizing neural machine translation with OPUS-MT. Language Resources and Evaluation, 58(2), 713-755.

Full Article Access: Available via Springer

docs.title: Available Models
docs.text:
To explore the extensive list of language pairs and pre-trained engines available within the Opus-MT framework, you can visit the official repository:

Official Model Registry: Opus-MT Trained Models on GitHub

docs.title: Testing OpusMT in a Pyhton script
docs.text:
We can use a simple python script (translateOpusMT.py to test OpusMT models. Before using the script, don't forget to install the requirements.txt:

transformers
torch
sentencepiece
sacremoses

The script is the following. Feel free to change the model to test and the sentence to translate.

from transformers import MarianMTModel, MarianTokenizer

def translate_text(text, model_name):
    """
    Translates a given string using a specific Opus-MT model.
    """
    # 1. Load the tokenizer and the model from Hugging Face
    tokenizer = MarianTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    model = MarianMTModel.from_pretrained(model_name)

    # 2. Tokenize the input text
    # The 'pt' return_tensors means we are using PyTorch
    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)

    # 3. Generate the translated tokens
    translated_tokens = model.generate(**inputs)

    # 4. Decode the tokens back into a human-readable string
    result = tokenizer.decode(translated_tokens[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
    return result

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Configuration
    model_id = "Helsinki-NLP/opus-mt-en-es"
    input_sentence = "This is a translation test using OpusMT"

    print("Loading model and performing translation...")

    # Execution
    translation = translate_text(input_sentence, model_id)

    # Output
    print("-" * 30)
    print(f"Original text:    {input_sentence}")
    print(f"Translated text:  {translation}")
    print("-" * 30)

As we don't want to translate isolate sentences one by one, the use of MTUOC-server to start a OpusMT model is very handy and the server adds several additional features.

docs.title: 2. Starting OpusMT in MTUOC-server
docs.text:
To start the MTUOC-server using a model from OpusMT we need to configure 2 files:

config-server.yaml

We simply need to set the MTEngine field to OpusMT and the model's config file in the model_config

MTengine: OpusMT
model_config: ./opus-mt-en-es/config-OpusMT.yaml
...

config-OpusMT.yaml

OpusMT:
  #model_path: Helsinki-NLP/opus-mt-en-es
  model_path: "./opus-mt-en-es"
  beam_size: 5
  num_hypotheses: 5
  multilingual_prefix: ""
  device: cuda
  #cuda cpu

In model_path we need to state the model we want to use. You can see the full list of models here. Most of the models are bilingual, that is, they can translate from one source language to one target language. For bilingual models we state multilingual_prefix: "", that is, no multilingual prefix. In OpusMT, however, some models are multilingual, meaning that they can translate from and to several languages. In this case we use the multilingual_prefix in the form of >>id<< (where id is a valid target language ID of the model.) For example, >>ca<< to translate to Catalan.

The parameters beam_search and num_hypotheses are related to the number of translation candidates the model will provide for each source sentence.

You may need to install the requirements. If you plan to use only OpusMT you don't need to install the whole set of requirements for MTUOC-server, but the specific ones that are in the requirements-OpusMT.txt file. The requirements are:

regex
pyyaml
ftfy
huggingface_hub
requests
lxml
transformers
torch
sentencepiece
sacremoses
flask
waitress
websocket-client

docs.title: What is a Virtual Environment?
docs.text: A Virtual Environment is an isolated workspace for a Python project. It allows you to install specific versions of libraries (dependencies) for a particular project without affecting the global Python installation of your operating system.

docs.title: Why should you use one?
docs.text:
When working with Machine Translation, you might need different versions of libraries like OpenNMT, transformers, or flask for different projects.

  • Avoid Conflicts: Prevents issues when two projects require different versions of the same library.
  • Clean System: Keeps your operating system's Python environment lean and stable.

Reproducibility: Makes it easier to share your project requirements with others (e.g., via a requirements.txt file).

docs.title: Working with venv in Linux
docs.text: Follow these steps to manage your environments via the Terminal.

docs.title: 1. Creating the Environment
docs.text:
Navigate to your project folder and run the following command. We usually name the environment folder .venv or env:

python3 -m venv env

This creates a directory named env containing a copy of the Python binary and a local site-packages folder. You can name the environment as you want, for example envMTUOC:

python3 -m venv envMTUOC

docs.title: 2. Activating the Environment
docs.text:
Before installing any library, you must "enter" the environment:

source env/bin/activate

or whatever other name you used to create the environent:

source envMTUOC/bin/activate

These commands are in the case when the virtual environment directory is in the working directory. If it is elsewhere, use the full paths.

How do you know it worked? Your Terminal prompt will now show the name of the environment in parentheses at the beginning of the line, like this: (env) user@computer:~/project$

docs.title: 3. Installing Packages
docs.text:
Once activated, any package you install using pip will stay inside that isolated folder:

python3 -m pip install packagename

To install all the requirements in the requirements.txt file write:

python3 -m pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: 4. Deactivating the Environment
docs.text:
When you are finished working on your project, you can exit the virtual environment and return to the system's default Python by simply typing:

deactivate

docs.title: Installing Virtall and portable version for Windows
docs.text:
To install Virtaal you can go to the following site: https://virtaal.translatehouse.org/download.html

I have prepared a portable version of Virtaal for Windows. Portable means that you can download and run the virtaal.exe program form the unzipped folder without need of installing it. You can download the portable version here: https://github.com/mtuoc/VirtaalPortable/releases/tag/0.7.1

docs.title: Configuration files
docs.text:
The first time you run the program several configuration files are created. These configuration files are necessary to set up some features of the program. When I started the Windows Portable version the configuration files where created at: C:\Users\USER\AppData\Roaming\Virtaal where USER is the actual user name. Please, note that by default this folder is hidden so to see it in the File Browser you should set Hidden files to be visible. 

You will see the following configuration files:

virtaal.ini

plugins.ini

terminology.ini

tm.ini

weblook.ini

docs.title: Configuration of a MTUOC MT system using the Moses protocol
docs.text:
Virtaal is not directly compatible with the MTUOC-server, but the nice thing of MTUOC-server is that it can be stated simulatin other server's protocols. So, if we start a MTUOC-server selecting the Moses protocol (in config-server.yaml change MTUOCServer-type to Moses). Start the MTUOC-server and then you'll be able to connect with Virtaal and automatically retrieve the machine translated segments.

Now edit the tm.ini config file of Virtaal and add the following information:

[moses]
fr->en = http://localhost:8080
en->ca = http://192.168.0.139:8000/

The fr->en is sometimes present by default. In our case we are translating from en to ca (change the languages to match your project) and the server is started in a computer with IP 192.168.0.139 and in the port 8000.

Now in Virtaal go to Edit > Preferences > Connectors and select Translation Memory and Config. Make sure Moses is selected there.

docs.title: Suggested reading
docs.text:
To learn about the MTUOC project, I suggest the following paper:

Antoni Oliver and Sergi Alvarez. 2023. Training and integration of neural machine translation with MTUOC. In Proceedings of the 1st Workshop on Open Community-Driven Machine Translation, pages 5–13, Tampere, Finland. European Association for Machine Translation.

To learn what the MTUOC-server is, I recommend reading this paper:

Antoni Oliver. (2025) MTUOC server: integrating several NMT and LLMs into professional translation workflows. In Proceedings of Machine Translation Summit XX: Volume 2

docs.title: 1. Overview of the MTUOC Project
docs.text:
The MTUOC project, developed at the Arts and Humanities Department of the Universitat Oberta de Catalunya (UOC), is designed to facilitate the training, use, and integration of neural and statistical machine translation (MT) systems, as well as LLMs for translation, in professional translation environments. While most MT toolkits are available under free licenses, their practical use presents several challenges:

  • Technical Skills: Toolkits often require medium-to-high programming knowledge (Python, Bash) and lack comprehensive documentation.
  • Integration: Trained engines cannot be easily integrated into Computer-Assisted Translation (CAT) tools.
  • Hardware Requirements: Training requires powerful GPUs or significant RAM, though simply translating with an already trained model requires far less.

MTUOC addresses these issues by providing well-documented scripts, graphical interfaces, and a highly configurable server. All project components are distributed under a free GNU GPL license, ensuring professionals and organizations can maintain technological independence and data confidentiality.

docs.title: 2. Key Components
docs.text:
The project provides a comprehensive ecosystem of tools covering the entire machine translation lifecycle:

  • Corpus Creation: Tools to convert TMX, SDLTM, and XLIFF files to tabular text. It includes programs for automatic document alignment using Hunalign, as well as comparable corpus mining using SBERT.
  • Corpus Cleaning and Combination: Scripts to remove HTML/XML tags, normalize characters, filter by length, and verify languages using fasttext. An algorith to clean and rescore large parallel corpora is also available. It also includes utilities to expand specialized corpora by retrieving similar segments from large general corpora based on language model perplexity.
  • Preprocessing and Training: Includes tokenizers, truecasers, and subword unit tools like BPE and SentencePiece. It provides training scripts and configuration files for Moses, Marian, OpenNMT, Eole, as well as guides and scripts to fine-tune LLMs for translation.
  • Free Engines: The project distributes pre-trained NMT models for several language pairs and domains.
  • Client programs: A desktop and we application to translate documents is also distributed. We're currently developing a postedition editor.

docs.title: 3. The MTUOC-server
docs.text: The MTUOC-server is a crucial component designed to solve the interoperability gap between machine translation engines and professional CAT tools.

docs.title: 3.1 Architecture
docs.text:
The server operates on a client-server model acting as a bridge. The workflow is straightforward:

  • The client (e.g., a CAT tool) sends a segment to the MTUOC-server.
  • The server preprocesses the segment (applying tokenization, truecasing, SentencePiece, etc.).
  • It forwards the processed segment to the underlying MT engine or LLM.
  • Upon receiving the translation, it post-processes the segment (detokenization, undoing subwords, etc.).
  • The final translated text is returned to the client.

docs.title: 3.2 Protocol Interoperability
docs.text: The standout feature of the MTUOC-server is its ability to communicate using multiple protocols, including MTUOC, Moses, ModernMT, OpenNMT, and NMTWizard. The server can mimic these protocols and new protocols can be easily added in the future. For instance, you can use a fast Marian neural engine, but configure the MTUOC-server to behave like a Moses server. This guarantees seamless integration with CAT tools.

docs.title: 3.3 Supported Systems and LLMs
docs.text:
The server supports a broad spectrum of translation technologies:

  • NMT Systems: Open-source engines like Marian, OpenNMT, Moses, OpusMT, NLLB, CTranslate2, and Apertium, as well as commercial APIs like DeepL and Google Translate.
  • Large Language Models (LLMs): The server integrates models such as Salamandra, Bloom, ChatGPT, DeepSeek, and Tower. Users can query these via API or by downloading full models from HuggingFace using the transformers library. Furthermore, the server allows users to specify custom translation prompts and use regular expressions to extract the translated text from the LLM's response.

docs.title: 3.4 Features for Professional Workflows
docs.text:
To meet professional standards, the server includes several advanced features:

  • XML/HTML Tag Restoration: Neural engines often struggle to place formatting tags correctly (e.g., in DOCX files). The server uses an algorithm that relies on word or subword alignments from the MT system to restore tags. If the MT system does not provide alignments, it can use external fast_align models.
  • Translation Memories: It can integrate translation memories and return a match if the similarity score exceeds a predefined threshold.
  • CAT Tool Plugins: Dedicated plugins are available for direct integration with OmegaT and RWS Trados Studio.

docs.title: 3.5 Hardware and Platform Compatibility
docs.text: The server is multiplatform, running on Linux, Windows, and macOS. While training MT systems requires powerful hardware, running the MTUOC-server for translation does not need a GPU. It can run on standard consumer computers, democratizing access to NMT and LLM technology while allowing translators to keep all client data completely confidential on their local machines.

docs.title: 4.0. Getting the MTUOC-server, versions and prerequisites
docs.text: To get the MTUOC server go to https://github.com/mtuoc/MTUOC-server. If you have a Python3 interpreter in your system, you can use the Python code and install the prerequisites (requirements.txt). Yo can also go to releases and get the binary version for your operating system.

docs.title: 4.1. From terminal
docs.text:
To start a MTUOC-server using the default config-server.yaml config file you can write in terminal:

python3 MTUOC-server.py

If you want to use a specific config file (for example config-server-OpusMT.yaml), use:

python3 MTUOC-server.py config-server-opusmt-eng-spa.yaml

To stop a MTUOC-server you can write in terminal:

python3 MTUOC-stop-server.py

or

python3 MTUOC-stop-server.py config-server-opusmt-eng-spa.yaml

A full engine can be downloaded and configured using MTUOC-recipes (take a look at the repository aoliverg/MTUOC-recipes). To download a recipe:

  1. If you already have the recipe as a file:

python3 MTUOC-downloader.py recipeAina-cat-eng.yaml

  1. Or using the link to the recipe:

python3 MTUOC-downloader.py https://huggingface.co/datasets/aoliverg/MTUOC-recipes/resolve/main/recipeAina-cat-eng.yaml `

docs.title: 4.2. Using the MTUOC-server manager
docs.text:
A GUI application is distributed to make the star/stop/configuration process of MTUOC-servers easier. The application is called MTUOC-server-manager.py. When you start the aplication a GUI interface is shown:

The application is organized in three tabs:

  • Control: where we can select the engine by its configuration file, start and stop the server and see the status of the server.
  • Test: where we can send sentences to translate.
  • Configuration Editor: where we can edit the basic configuration of the translation engine.
  • Aux. editor: where we can edit the required additional configuration files.
  • Recipes: where you can download full configured engines from recipes.

4.2.a. The Control tab

In this tab we can select one of the available server configurations files (these files should be placed in the same directory of the MTUOC-server-manager. We can select one of the available configuration and Start the server. In the console we can observe the relevant information of the server. We can use the Stop button to stop the server.

4.2.b. The Test tab

In the Test tab we can write or paste sentences or paragraph to test the started system

4.2.c. The Configuration Editor tab

When we click to the button LOAD SELECTED CONFIGURATION & LINKED MODEL CONFIG the configuration are loaded to two tabs: 1. Server Configuration (Main) and 2. Model configuration (Linked). We can observer and make changes to the configuration files and save the changes with the SAVE.... button in the bottom. Please, note that changes are not applied to the running server until we stop and start it again.

4.2.d. The Aux. Editor tab

This tab allow for the edition of any text file and it is intended for the edition of additional configuration files needed for some models. You can open any text file with the OPEN button, modify it, and save it with the SAVE AS button.

4.2.e. The Recipes tab

This tab allows for the downloading of full engines (including models, alignment models, truecasers, etc.) from a Hugging face repository of MTUOC recipes. By default the "aoliverg/MTUOC-recipes" repository is selected. If you change the repository, use the CONNECT/REFRESH BUTTON to update the information about the available recipes. The recipes can be filtered by a string, as in the example, to limit the number of recipes shown. Select the desired recipe and Donwload it using the DOWNLOAD SELECTED RECIPE button.

docs.title: Testing OpusMT in a Pyhton script
docs.text:
We can use a simple python script (translateOpusMT.py to test OpusMT models. Before using the script, don't forget to install the requirements.txt:

transformers
torch
sentencepiece
sacremoses

The script is the following. Feel free to change the model to test and the sentence to translate.

from transformers import MarianMTModel, MarianTokenizer

def translate_text(text, model_name):
    """
    Translates a given string using a specific Opus-MT model.
    """
    # 1. Load the tokenizer and the model from Hugging Face
    tokenizer = MarianTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    model = MarianMTModel.from_pretrained(model_name)

    # 2. Tokenize the input text
    # The 'pt' return_tensors means we are using PyTorch
    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)

    # 3. Generate the translated tokens
    translated_tokens = model.generate(**inputs)

    # 4. Decode the tokens back into a human-readable string
    result = tokenizer.decode(translated_tokens[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
    return result

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Configuration
    #This way the model is automatically downloaded
    #model_id = "Helsinki-NLP/opus-mt-en-es"
    #To use an already downloaded model
    model_id = "../../models/opus-mt-en-es"
    input_sentence = "This is a translation test using OpusMT"

    print("Loading model and performing translation...")

    # Execution
    translation = translate_text(input_sentence, model_id)

    # Output
    print("-" * 30)
    print(f"Original text:    {input_sentence}")
    print(f"Translated text:  {translation}")
    print("-" * 30)

As we don't want to translate isolate sentences one by one, the use of MTUOC-server to start a OpusMT model is very handy and the server adds several additional features.

docs.title: 2. Starting OpusMT in MTUOC-server
docs.text:
To start the MTUOC-server using a model from OpusMT we need to configure 2 files:

config-server-opusmt.yaml

We simply need to set the MTEngine field to OpusMT and state the path and name of the config file for the OpusMT model. We can also set a system_name, but this is not compulsory.

MTengine: OpusMT
#OpusMT, NLLB, HuggingFace, Ollama, ctranslate2, GoogleT, DeepL, Marian, Eole, Apertium...
system_name: OpusMT-eng-spa
model_config: config-model-opusmt-eng-spa.yaml

The rest of the configuration file is used to set the port, type of server, preprocessing, postprocessing and tranlation memory.

MTUOCServer:
  port: 8000
  type: MTUOC
  #one of MTUOC, Moses, ModernMT, OpenNMT, NMTWizard
  verbosity_level: 1
  #specific configuration when acting as ONMT server
  log_file: log.log

dopreprocess: False
preprocess_config: None
dotokenization: False
tokenization_config: None
dopostprocess: False
postprocess_config: None

TranslationMemory: 
  use: False
  memo: None
  minsim: 100

And then configure the model with the config-OpusMT.yaml file:

OpusMT:
  #model_path: Helsinki-NLP/opus-mt-en-es
  model_path: "./opus-mt-en-es"
  beam_size: 5
  num_hypotheses: 5
  multilingual_prefix: ""
  device: cuda
  #cuda cpu

In model_path we need to state the model we want to use. You can see the full list of models here. We can set the model_path as model_path: Helsinki-NLP/opus-mt-en-es and then the first time we use the model it will be automatically downloaded from HuggingFace. We can also set as a path to a model already downloaded in our system, as in model_path: "./opus-mt-en-es". Most of the models are bilingual, that is, they can translate from one source language to one target language. For bilingual models we state multilingual_prefix: "", that is, no multilingual prefix. In OpusMT, however, some models are multilingual, meaning that they can translate from and to several languages. In this case we use the multilingual_prefix in the form of >>id<< (where id is a valid target language ID of the model.) For example, >>ca<< to translate to Catalan.

The parameters beam_search and num_hypotheses are related to the number of translation candidates the model will provide for each source sentence.

You may need to install the requirements. If you plan to use only OpusMT you don't need to install the whole set of requirements for MTUOC-server, but the specific ones that are in the requirements-OpusMT.txt file. The requirements are:

#GENERAL
pyyaml
requests
flask
waitress
websocket-client
sentencepiece
sacremoses
ftfy
protobuf

#OPUS MT
#pytorch
torch

Now you can start the MTUOC-server, either in the terminal:

python3 MTUOC-server.py config-server-opusmt.yaml

And the following information will appear in the terminal:

...or using the MTUOC-server-manager, selecting the model in the Control tab, and clicking the button Start.

To test the system, if you started the system in the terminal, you can start the program MTUOC-test-server.py with the configuration file as a parameter:

python3 MTUOC-test-server.py config-server-opusmt.yaml

the following application will appear where you can write or paste a text and clic the button Translate.

Or if you started the system with MTUOC-server-manager you can go to the tab Test and write or paste a text there:

Once you finish using the server, you may want to stop the server using the Stop button in the MTUOC-server-manager or writing in the terminal:

python3 MTUOC-stop-server.py config-server-opusmt.yaml

docs.title: 3. Example MTUOC recipes
docs.text:
You have a couple of recipes that can serve as example for using OpusMT and adapt them to your own needs:

  • recipe-OpusMT-noprepost-eng-spa.yaml Basic recipe: only the OpusMT model, with no truecasing and no automatic resauration
  • TODO: advanced recipe

You can use this recipe in two ways:

  • Using the MTUOC-downloader:

Copy the Download link to the recipe from HuggingFace and write:

python3 MTUOC-downloader.py https://huggingface.co/datasets/aoliverg/MTUOC-recipes/resolve/main/recipe-OpusMT-noprepost-eng-spa.yaml

  • Using the MTUOC-server-manager, from the Recipes tab you can download the recipes.

Some of the configuration files may be placed in the MTUOC-server directory, and some other, in the model folder.

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
NLLB (No Language Left Behind) is a multilingual neural machine translation model. This means that the same model can translate from and to many languages (in the case of the NLLB model, more than 200; the complete list of languages is provided in the appendix). The MTUOC-server can deploy this model and performs the download automatically. However, when launching the model, we must specify which language pair we require (although if we change it later, it will not be necessary to download the model again).

To learn more about this model, you can read the following article:

NLLB Team. Scaling neural machine translation to 200 languages. Nature 630, 841–846 (2024).

docs.title: Testing NLLB in a Pyhton script
docs.text:
As we did with OpusMT, we can use a script to test a NLLB model. You can change the size of the model (see below), the source and target languages (see the list of available models in Appendix 1) and the sentence to translate. The script is translateNLLB.py:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM

def translate_nllb(text, model_name, source_lang, target_lang):
    """
    Translates text using the NLLB model.
    """
    # 1. Load the tokenizer and model
    # NLLB uses Auto classes for easier loading
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    model = AutoModelForSeq2SeqLM.from_pretrained(model_name)

    # 2. Prepare the input and specify the source language
    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)

    # 3. Generate the translation specifying the target language code
    # forced_bos_token_id tells the model which language to translate into
    forced_bos_token_id = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(target_lang)
    translated_tokens = model.generate(
        **inputs, 
        forced_bos_token_id=forced_bos_token_id
    )

    # 4. Decode the output
    result = tokenizer.decode(translated_tokens[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
    return result

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Model configuration
    #If you want to download from HuggingFace
    #model_id = "facebook/nllb-200-distilled-600M"
    #If you want to use a local version
    model_id = "../../models/nllb-200-distilled-600M"
    # Language Codes: 
    # Spanish: spa_Latn | English: eng_Latn | Catalan: cat_Latn
    src_code = "eng_Latn" 
    tgt_code = "spa_Latn"

    input_sentence = "This is a translated sentence using NLLB."

    print(f"Loading {model_id}...")

    translation = translate_nllb(input_sentence, model_id, src_code, tgt_code)

    print("-" * 30)
    print(f"Source ({src_code}): {input_sentence}")
    print(f"Target ({tgt_code}): {translation}")
    print("-" * 30)

And the requirements.txt:

transformers
torch

docs.title: 2. Starting NLLB in MTUOC-server
docs.text:
To start the MTUOC-server using an NLLB model we need to configure 2 files:

config-server-SalamandraTA.yaml

We simply need to set the MTEngine field to NLLB and to set de model's config file. The field system_name is not compulsory.

MTengine: HuggingFace
#download, Marian, Eole, OpusMT, NLLB, HuggingFace, Ollama, GoogleT, DeepL
system_name: SalamandraTA
model_config: config-SalamandraTA.yaml

MTUOCServer:
  port: 8000
  type: MTUOC
  #one of MTUOC, Moses, ModernMT, OpenNMT, NMTWizard
  verbosity_level: 1
  #specific configuration when acting as ONMT server
  log_file: log.log

dopreprocess: False
preprocess_config: None
dotokenization: False
tokenization_config: None
dopostprocess: False
postprocess_config: None

TranslationMemory: 
#path to memory or None
  Path: None  
  sl: es
  tl: en

config-model-NLLB.yaml

We have to configure the following parameters:

NLLB:
  #model: facebook/nllb-200-distilled-600M
  model: "./nllb-200-distilled-600M"
  #A HuggingFace repository folder for the  model or full or relative path to a local ./NLLB-FT-model
  src_lang: eng_Latn
  tgt_lang: spa_Latn
  device: gpu
  beam_size: 5
  num_hypotheses: 5

Firstly, we must state the model. The NLLB model is distributed in several sizes:

  • facebook/nllb-200-distilled-600M
  • facebook/nllb-200-1.3B
  • facebook/nllb-200-distilled-1.3B
  • facebook/nllb-200-3.3B

You can also set a local path (both full or relative) to an already downloaded model or a fine-tuned one.

The source language code (scr_lang) and target language code (tgt_lang) should be set, following the available languages and codes on Appendix 1.

In the field device we should set whether we want to use the cpu or gpu.

The parameters beam_search and num_hypotheses are related to the number of translation candidates the model will provide for each source sentence.

docs.title: 1. Installation
docs.text:
• Download the plugin: MtuocTranslatePlugin-0.1.0.jar.

  • Place the JAR file in the corresponding directory for your operating system:

  • Windows: C:\Program Files\OmegaT\plugin

  • Linux: $HOME/.omegat/plugin
  • Mac: ~/Library/Preferences/OmegaT/plugins

Note: These paths may vary depending on your specific OmegaT installation and configuration.

docs.title: 2. Plugin configuration
docs.text:
Once the JAR file is in the correct folder, launch OmegaT and follow these steps:

  1. Navigate to Options > Preferences.

  1. In the sidebar, select Machine Translation.

  2. Select MTUOC MT Engine and click Configure.

  1. Enter the details for your available system (either a local or remote server).

  2. Click Test connection to verify the setup. If the configuration is correct, the message "Server connection OK" will be displayed.

The MT engine is now active, and suggestions will appear automatically in the Machine Translation pane.

For further details on integration settings, please consult the OmegaT manual: Machine translations

docs.title: Downloading the plug-in
docs.text: Download the plug-in for your version of Trados Studio. At the moment we offer a plug-in for each of the following versions: 2019, 2021 and 2022. Once downloaded, unzip the file.

docs.title: Installing the plug-in
docs.text:
Uninstall any previous versions of the MTUOC Trados plugin before installing the new version. Open the uncompressed folder and doble-clic to the file:

The following interface will appear:

Choose the correct version of Trados Studio and the folder (for all the domain computers, current user or all users) where the plug-in will be installed. Clic on the Next button. Once the installation is completed click on the Finnish button.

docs.title: Unsigned plug-in warning
docs.text:
Depending on the version of the plug-in, when opening Trados Studio, a warning indicating that the plug-in is unsigned may appear. As the following one:

Answer Yes.

The plug-ins are signed by Trados and we ask for the signature once a new version of the plug-in is released. As this process takes some days, you may eventually find unsigned plug-ins.

docs.title: Plug-in configuration
docs.text:
To configure the plug-in, once opened a project go to Project Configuration and select Translation memory and machine translation, either for All language combination or for a given languae combination. Then select Use and clic on MTUOC-MACHINE TRANSLATION and this plug-in will appear:

Unselect it, in case it's selected, and then clic on Settings. The following interface will appear:

docs.title: General tab
docs.text: In this tab we can set the IP (or server name) and Port of the MTUOC server. No language pair is provided as each IP and port serves an unique language pair. The plug-in, nor Trados, make no language verification, so the user should know the language pair of the IP and port combination.

docs.title: Connection test tab
docs.text: In this tab we can perform a connection test to make sure the server works properly. Write or paste a source sentence and clic on the button Send

docs.title: Advanced tab
docs.text:
In this tab some advanced configuration can be set:

  • Cache - Keep the cache (days): to set how many days the cache sould be kept. You can also delete the cache at any moment clicking on the Delete cache button.
  • Translation configuration:
  • Score: the score given to the machine translation. This allows to combine machine translation with translation memories. The machine translation target will be selected of the best translation memory match is under this score.
  • Description: the description string that will be set in the SDLXLIFF file of the project for the translated segments coming from machine translation. The following values can be set: Unknown, TM, Machine translation, Alignment, Context TM, Adaptative Machine Translation, Nmt and AutomaticTranslation.

To accept the configuration the button Accept can be clicked in any tab.

To close and accept the project settings click on the button OK.

Once the configuration is done, the source segments will be translated with the MTUOC server set in the configuration.

docs.title: Using the Plugin Installer
docs.text:
Your Trados Studio distribution comes with a program that facilitates the installation and uninstallation of plugins. The program is called Sdl.Community.SdlPluginInstaller.exe. Depending on the version this program can be located in different folders on your computer.

For example, in Trados Studio 2022 the program most probably is located at: Program Files (x86)> Trados > Plugin installer. Once located, double-click on it and the following (or similar) interface would appear:

In this interface, be sure the correct version of Trados is selected in Filter by Studio version:". The plugin will appear and you can uninstall it clicking on the Uninstall** button at the right of the plugin.

For other versions of Trados Studio, for example the 2021, the program can be located at: Program Files (x86)> SDL > Plugin installer.

docs.title: Manually uninstalling the plugin
docs.text:
You can also manually uninstall the plugin deleting some files and folders in your computer. The location of these files and foldes may depend on the version and the installation.

For Trados Studio 2022, try locating the following folder. Under the User's folder, locate the AppData folder and go to \AppData\Roaming\Trados\Trados Studio\17\Plugins. Under this folder, you'll find two subfolders: Packages and Unpacked. In the Unpacked folder, delete the SDL.Trados.MTUOC folder. If this plugin is the only one installed in the system, you can safely delete all the Unpacked folder. Then, go to the Packages folder and delete all the files and folders corresponding to this plugin.

For Trados Studio 2021, follow the same procedure in the folder \AppData\Roaming\SDL\SDL Trados Studio\16\Plugins.

For Trados Studio 2019, follow the same procedure in the folder \AppData\Roaming\SDL\SDL Trados Studio\15\Plugins.

docs.title: Manual installation of the plugin
docs.text:
The plugin can manually installed following these steps:

  1. Download the zip file corresponding to your version of Trados Studio.

  2. Uncompress the zip file.

  3. Copy all the files and folders to the following folders, depending on the version of Trados Studio:
  4. For Trados Studio 2019, on your user's folder in \AppData\Roaming\SDL\SDL Trados Studio\15\Plugins\Packages
  5. For Trados Studio 2021, on your user's folder in \AppData\Roaming\SDL\SDL Trados Studio\16\Plugins\Packages
  6. For Trados Studio 2022, on your user's folder in \AppData\Roaming\Trados\Trados Studio\17\Plugins\Packages
